planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in shade; Cretan brake fern is one of the easiest and most adaptable ferns for shaded gardens in zones 8-12, tolerating a wide range of soil types and moisture levels.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Cretan brake fern is one of the most widely grown ornamental ferns in warm-climate gardens, with elegant, finger-like fronds divided into narrow, pale green leaflets on dark, wiry stalks. The many cultivars offer striking variation with white-striped or crested leaflet tips. Highly adaptable to both full shade and indirect light, and tolerant of moderate drought once established. Spreads by spores to self-establish in appropriate shaded garden conditions and is one of the best ferns for shaded beds, walkways, and under mature trees in the warm Southeast. Hardy to about 20-25°F; recovers quickly from light frost damage.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in humus-rich, moist, well-drained soil in shade; silver lace fern is excellent in sheltered, frost-free positions in zones 9-12, or in containers brought indoors in winter in colder zones.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in full shade to bright indirect light; silver lace fern is one of the most ornamental small ferns available, with intricate fronds where each leaflet is dramatically marked with a broad central stripe of silvery white against dark green margins. The cultivar Victoriae has an especially bold silver central band. Grows to 10-18 inches and makes an excellent groundcover for deeply shaded spots or a container specimen. In zone 9, grow in a sheltered, frost-protected position or in a container moved indoors below 32°F. Native to tropical and subtropical Asia and the Pacific.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in shade; rough maidenhair fern is more drought tolerant than most adiantums and better adapted to humid subtropical conditions.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; rough maidenhair fern is broadly adapted and heat-tolerant, native to tropical Africa, Asia, and Australia, and far better suited to humid subtropical conditions of zone 8-11 than the delicate northern maidenhair fern. New fronds emerge in shades of coppery-pink or bronze before maturing to bright fresh green. The distinctive palmate frond arrangement with leaflets resembling a spread hand is elegant and recognizable. More tolerant of occasional dryness than other maidenhair ferns, making it more forgiving in garden conditions where desiccation is a common maidenhair killer.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, acidic to neutral soil in shade; tree selaginella is one of the best selaginellas for outdoor shaded garden use in zones 7-10, forming attractive miniature fern-like clumps that spread slowly.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in full shade; tree selaginella is an arching, bush-like fern ally from China that resembles a miniature tree fern with delicate, lacy branches of bright green scale-like foliage arranged in flattened, frond-like planes. Unlike prostrate mosses, tree selaginella forms an upright, branching structure 12-18 inches tall that creates ornamental textural accent in deeply shaded areas. Excellent as a groundcover under large ferns or hostas, or as a container specimen for shaded patios. The iridescent blue-green sheen on the leaves in certain light conditions makes it particularly attractive. More cold-tolerant than most selaginellas and one of the most reliable for outdoor shade garden use in the Southeast.

planting.note: Plant container specimens in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; Brazilian tree fern forms a dramatic architectural accent with new fronds emerging in coppery-red before maturing to dark green.

directions: Plant in consistently moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Brazilian tree fern develops a short, scaly trunk over time and produces striking architectural fronds up to 3 feet long with a distinctive ladder-like arrangement of leaflets. New croziers and expanding fronds emerge in vivid copper to red tones before maturing to dark, glossy green. The contrast between the dark mature fronds and the red new growth is especially dramatic in spring. It thrives in sheltered, humid, shaded positions in zones 8b-11, excellent at the base of a building wall or among large tropical perennials in a sheltered courtyard garden.
